ui.heroBody: Work through short lessons with real Rust code. Read the example, predict the output, and answer a quick quiz right in your browser.
ui.tapItemsBelow: Tap items below to build your answer.
ui.keyboardHint: Tip: use the arrow keys to move between lessons.
ui.incorrectReasonFallback: That choice does not match what Rust allows in this example.

01_hello.title: Hello, world!
01_hello.description: This is the standard first Rust program. `println!` is a macro that prints a line to standard output. The `!` matters: in Rust, macros use `!` because they expand into code before the program runs. When you write something like `println!("{name}")`, Rust fills in the value of `name` inside the string using formatting rules checked at compile time.

q1.question: What does this program print? (Type the exact output.)

q2.question: What is println! ?

02_variables.title: Variables and mutability
02_variables.description:
`let` creates a binding.

```rust
let y = 10;
```

This means: make a variable named `y` and bind it to `10`.

```rust
let mut y = 10;
y = 11;
```

`mut` allows reassignment. Without `mut`, `y = 11;` is an error.

```rust
let y: i32 = 10;
```

`:` introduces a type annotation. Here, `y` is explicitly marked as `i32`.

```rust
let y = y + 1;
```

This does not mutate the old `y`. It creates a new `y` by shadowing the previous one.

q2.question: If you write `let y: i32 = 10;` without `mut`, which extra line still compiles?
explanations: This tries to assign a new value to `y`, but `y` is immutable because it was not declared with `mut`.
This is also a mutation. `+=` still changes the existing variable, so it needs `mut`.
This works because it creates a new variable named `y` that shadows the old one instead of mutating it.
`i32` does not have a `.set(...)` method, so this fails before mutability even matters.
q2.answerReason: The correct choice is `let y = y + 1;` because it shadows the original binding with a new one.

03_ownership.title: Ownership and borrowing
03_ownership.description: Ownership is one of Rust's core rules. Every value has exactly one owner, which is the variable responsible for it. When ownership moves to another variable or function, the old variable can no longer be used. Borrowing is different: when you pass `&value`, you let other code look at the value without taking ownership of it. This is why borrowing lets you keep using the original variable afterward, while moving does not.

q2.question: After `takes_ownership(owned)` is called, what happens if we try to use `owned` again?

04_control_flow.title: Control flow
04_control_flow.description: Control flow is how a program decides what to do next. Rust uses `if` and `else` for branching, `while` for repeating while a condition stays true, and `for` for iterating over ranges or collections. A range written as `0..3` is half-open, which means it starts at `0` and stops before `3`. So it produces `0`, `1`, and `2`, but not `3`. This is a common source of off-by-one mistakes when you are learning the language.

q2.question: How many times does `for x in 0..3` iterate?

05_functions.title: Functions and expressions
05_functions.description: Functions in Rust declare the types of their parameters and, when needed, the type they return. One important grammar rule is that Rust distinguishes between expressions and statements. An expression produces a value. A statement performs an action. In a block like `{ a + b }`, the final line has no semicolon, so it is treated as an expression and its value becomes the value of the whole block. That is why a Rust function can return a value without writing the word `return`.

q2.question: Why does `fn add(a, b) -> i32 { a + b }` return `a + b` without a `return` keyword?
options: All Rust functions auto-return their last variable
The last expression without a semicolon is the block's value
The compiler inserts a return at the end

06_strings.title: Strings: String vs &str
06_strings.description: Rust has two common string forms. `&str` is a string slice: a borrowed view into text stored somewhere else, such as a string literal. `String` is an owned, growable string stored on the heap. You use `&str` when you only need to read text, and `String` when you need ownership or want to modify and build new text. The macro `format!` creates and returns a new `String` instead of printing it.

q2.question: Which statement is true?

07_tuples_arrays.title: Tuples and arrays
07_tuples_arrays.description: Tuples and arrays both hold multiple values, but they serve different purposes. A tuple can store values of different types, such as an integer and a string together. You often unpack a tuple with destructuring syntax like `let (a, b) = pair;`. An array stores multiple values of the same type, and its length is fixed at compile time. Rust treats that fixed size as part of the array's type.

q2.question: Fill in the blank to destructure the tuple.

08_enums.title: Enums and match
08_enums.description: Enums let a value be one of several variants — and each variant can carry its own data. `match` is the idiomatic way to react to each case, and it must be exhaustive.

q2.question: Which statement about `match` on an enum is true?
options: match needs exhaustiveness only for primitive types
All variants must share the same data type

09_structs.title: Structs and methods
09_structs.description: Structs bundle named fields. An `impl` block attaches methods (those that take `&self`) and associated functions (those that don't).

q2.question: What does an `impl` block do for a struct?
options: Hides all fields from outside code

10_closures_basic.title: Closures
10_closures_basic.description: A closure is an anonymous function value. It can be passed around and called. Parameter and return types can usually be inferred from how it's used.

q2.question: Which best describes `let add = |a: i32, b: i32| a + b;` ?

11_closures_capture.title: Closures: capturing state
11_closures_capture.description: Closures capture variables from the surrounding scope. A closure that mutates captured state must itself be declared `mut`.

q2.question: Why must `increment` be declared `mut`?

12_iter_map.title: Iterators: map
12_iter_map.description: `Iterator::map` lazily transforms each element. Nothing actually happens until the iterator is consumed — for example, by `collect()`.

q2.question: Which iterator method transforms each element? Fill in the blank.

13_iter_filter.title: Iterators: filter
13_iter_filter.description: `Iterator::filter` keeps only the elements for which the predicate returns true. Like `map`, it's lazy until you collect or consume it.

q2.question: When does `filter`'s predicate run?
options: Once per element, eagerly into a Vec
Lazily, only when the iterator is consumed
Filter requires a boolean array as input

14_iter_fold.title: Iterators: fold
14_iter_fold.description: `fold` reduces an iterator to a single value, threading an accumulator through each step. The first argument is the initial accumulator.

q2.question: Which statement is true about `fold`?
options: It takes an initial accumulator and combines elements
Its initial value must always be 0

15_iter_sum.title: Iterators: sum and max
15_iter_sum.description: `sum()` and `max()` are convenience methods that consume an iterator. `sum()` needs to know the result type, so it often requires a type annotation.

q2.question: The snippet below has one line that won't compile. Which line is it?

16_traits_basic.title: Traits
16_traits_basic.description: A trait is a set of methods a type can implement. Other code can then operate on "any type that implements this trait."

q2.question: What does `impl Greet for Person` do?

17_traits_default.title: Traits: default methods
17_traits_default.description: A trait method can have a default body. Implementors may override it, or fall back to the default by simply not defining it.

q2.question: Why does `Dog` print `dog: ...`?
options: Dog uses the default `sound()` because it doesn't override it

18_traits_derive.title: Traits: derive
18_traits_derive.description: `#[derive(...)]` auto-generates implementations of common traits like `Debug`, `Clone`, and `PartialEq` — provided every field also implements them.

q2.question: What does `#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq)]` do?

19_generics_basic.title: Generics
19_generics_basic.description: Generic functions take a type parameter, optionally bounded by traits. `T: PartialOrd + Copy` means "any type that can be ordered and trivially copied."

q2.question: Which is the correct syntax for a bounded generic?

20_lifetimes_basic.title: Lifetimes
20_lifetimes_basic.description: A lifetime parameter `'a` ties references together so the compiler can guarantee no reference outlives its data. The function below promises that the result lives at least as long as both inputs.

q2.question: What does the `'a` in `fn longest<'a>(x: &'a str, y: &'a str) -> &'a str` mean?
options: The returned reference lives at least as long as both inputs

21_lifetimes_struct.title: Lifetimes in structs
21_lifetimes_struct.description: If a struct holds a reference, it must declare a lifetime. Otherwise the compiler can't tell how long the reference must remain valid.

q2.question: Which line below has the missing lifetime that prevents this from compiling?

22_result_basic.title: Result and match
22_result_basic.description: `Result<T, E>` is the standard "this might fail" type. It has two variants — `Ok(T)` and `Err(E)` — and you destructure them with `match`.

q2.question: Which is true about Result?
options: It's an enum with Ok and Err variants

23_option_basic.title: Option and unwrap_or
23_option_basic.description: `Option<T>` is `Some(T)` or `None`. `unwrap_or(default)` gives you the inner value, or a default if it's `None`.

q2.question: What does `unwrap_or("none")` do when called on `None`?

24_error_propagation.title: Error propagation with ?
24_error_propagation.description: The `?` operator returns early from a function if the value is an `Err`, otherwise unwraps the `Ok`. It only works in functions that return `Result` (or `Option`).

q2.question: Order what happens when `parse_and_double("21")` is called, in execution order.
options: Parsing succeeds; `?` unwraps Ok(21) into n

25_panic_unwrap.title: panic, unwrap, expect
25_panic_unwrap.description: `unwrap()` and `expect(...)` extract the inner value of an `Option` or `Result`, but **panic** at runtime if the value is `None` / `Err`. Use them when failure is genuinely impossible — or you want to fail loudly.

q2.question: What happens if `value.unwrap()` is called when `value` is `None`?

26_async_basic.title: async / await
26_async_basic.description: `async fn` returns a future that doesn't run until something polls it. Here, `pollster::block_on` is a tiny blocking executor that drives the future to completion.

q2.question: What does calling an `async fn` actually do?
options: Returns a future that needs to be awaited or polled

27_box_basic.title: Smart pointers: Box
27_box_basic.description: `Box<T>` puts a value on the heap. Useful when you need an owned value of unknown size at compile time, or to keep a large value out of stack frames.

q2.question: Where does Box<T> store its data?

28_rc_basic.title: Smart pointers: Rc
28_rc_basic.description: `Rc<T>` enables multiple owners by counting references. The data is dropped only when the last `Rc` goes out of scope. Single-threaded only — use `Arc` across threads.

q2.question: Which best describes Rc<T>?

29_refcell_basic.title: Smart pointers: RefCell
29_refcell_basic.description: `RefCell<T>` lets you mutate data through an immutable reference, with the borrow rules checked at **runtime** instead of compile time. Pair with `Rc` for shared mutable state.

q2.question: Order the steps the program performs.
options: Create a RefCell holding the value 5
Borrow mutably and add 10 to the inner value
Drop the mutable borrow when its scope ends
Borrow immutably to print the final value

30_macros_basic.title: Macros: built-in
30_macros_basic.description: Macros end with `!`. They're expanded at **compile time** into real code, which is how `println!`, `vec!`, and `format!` accept variadic arguments and check format strings statically.

q2.question: When do macros run?
options: At compile time, expanding into code

31_macros_custom.title: Macros: macro_rules!
31_macros_custom.description: `macro_rules!` defines a *declarative* macro: a set of pattern → expansion rules. Patterns can match nothing, an expression, a list, etc.

q2.question: What does `macro_rules!` define?

32_unsafe_basic.title: Unsafe Rust
32_unsafe_basic.description: Inside `unsafe { }`, you can do things the compiler can't verify: dereference raw pointers, call unsafe functions, implement unsafe traits. The borrow checker is still active — `unsafe` opens *additional* powers, not a free-for-all.

q2.question: Which is true about `unsafe { }`?
options: Inside it you can dereference raw pointers
It makes Rust as memory-safe as Java
Raw pointers are checked at runtime

33_vec_push.title: Vectors and push
33_vec_push.description: `Vec<T>` is Rust's growable array type. It stores elements on the heap, tracks length separately from capacity, and `push()` appends a new item to the end.

q2.question: Fill in the method used to append `3` to the vector.

34_option_match.title: Option with match guards
34_option_match.description: A `match` arm can include an extra `if` condition called a guard. Guards let you refine patterns like `Some(n)` into more specific cases without nesting another `if`.

q2.question: Why does `Some(25)` print `big`?
options: Because every Some value is treated as big
Because the `Some(n) if n > 10` guard matches
Because Option automatically unwraps values over 10

35_arc_mutex.title: Arc and Mutex
35_arc_mutex.description: `Arc<T>` provides shared ownership across threads, while `Mutex<T>` gives mutable access one lock holder at a time. They are commonly paired for shared synchronized state.

q2.question: Order what happens in this program.
options: Create an Arc holding a Mutex with 0 inside
Clone the Arc so two owners point to the same Mutex
Lock the Mutex and add 2 to the inner value
Lock again to print the final count

36_iter_enumerate.title: Iterators: enumerate
36_iter_enumerate.description: `enumerate()` wraps an iterator and yields `(index, item)` pairs. Because each iteration now returns a tuple, the loop pattern must destructure it with parentheses.

q2.question: The snippet below has one line that won't compile. Which line is it?
